questions.question: What is the primary benefit of using Notion's comments and discussions for a team?

options.text: It forces all team members to work in real‑time.
options.explanation: Comments are designed for asynchronous collaboration, not real‑time work.

options.text: It reduces the need for numerous meetings and real‑time chats.
options.explanation: The article states that using comments lets teams work asynchronously, cutting down on meetings.

options.text: It replaces the need for any written documentation.
options.explanation: Comments supplement documentation, they don’t replace it.

options.text: It automatically generates project timelines.
options.explanation: Commenting does not create timelines; it’s a communication tool.

questions.question: Where should you place general feedback on a Notion database page?

options.text: In the discussion section at the top of the page.
options.explanation: The article specifies that the discussion section is for general feedback.

options.text: In a comment attached to a specific block.
options.explanation: Specific‑block comments are for tactical feedback, not general.

options.text: In the page’s title.
options.explanation: The title isn’t meant for feedback.

options.text: In the sidebar’s Inbox.
options.explanation: Inbox shows notifications, not a place to write feedback.

questions.question: How does Notion notify a teammate that they’ve been mentioned in a comment?

options.text: It sends an email only if the teammate has email notifications enabled.
options.explanation: While email may be sent, the primary notification is in‑app.

options.text: The teammate receives an in‑app notification and can reply directly from the Inbox.
options.explanation: The article says a mention triggers a notification that appears in the Inbox, where they can respond.

options.text: Their name appears highlighted in the comment but no notification is sent.
options.explanation: Mentioning does generate a notification, not just highlighting.

options.text: A pop‑up window appears on their screen immediately.
options.explanation: Notion uses the Inbox system, not intrusive pop‑ups.

questions.question: If you want to suggest a different wording for a piece of text, which visual cue does the article recommend?

options.text: Underline the text in blue.
options.explanation: The article does not mention blue underlines.

options.text: Highlight the text in yellow.
options.explanation: Yellow highlighting isn’t the suggested method.

options.text: Highlight the text in red.
options.explanation: The article explicitly says to highlight text in red for clear visual feedback.

options.text: Add an emoji next to the text.
options.explanation: Emojis are not described as the visual feedback method for wording suggestions.

questions.question: What recent UI change does the article highlight regarding notification management?

options.text: The "Updates" menu was renamed to "Inbox".
options.explanation: The article notes that "Updates" is now "Inbox" with a refreshed look and workflow.

options.text: The "Inbox" menu was merged into the "Updates" menu.
options.explanation: It’s the opposite; Updates became Inbox.

options.text: A new "Notifications" tab was added beside "Inbox".
options.explanation: No such addition is mentioned.

options.text: All notifications now appear as pop‑up alerts.
options.explanation: The change concerns the sidebar menu, not pop‑up alerts.

questions.question: Which of the following statements best describes the relationship between the "discussion" section and "comments" on a Notion page?

options.text: Both are used interchangeably for any type of feedback.
options.explanation: They serve different scopes; discussion is for general feedback, comments are block‑specific.

options.text: The discussion section is for high‑level feedback, while comments target specific blocks for tactical input.
options.explanation: The article differentiates the two: discussion = general, comments = tactical.

options.text: Comments can only be added by the page owner, whereas the discussion section is open to all collaborators.
options.explanation: Both can be used by any collaborator with edit/comment rights.

options.text: Only the discussion section supports @‑mentions.
options.explanation: You can @‑mention teammates in any comment, not just in the discussion section.

questions.question: Why might a team choose to use Notion’s comment system instead of scheduling a meeting?

options.text: Because comments automatically generate a meeting agenda.
options.explanation: Comments do not create agendas; they provide asynchronous feedback.

options.text: Because comments allow asynchronous input, letting people contribute when their schedule permits, reducing meeting overhead.
options.explanation: The article emphasizes asynchronous collaboration as a way to cut down on meetings.

options.text: Because comments are visible only to the comment author, ensuring privacy.
options.explanation: Comments are visible to anyone with access to the page, not private.

options.text: Because using comments disables the need for any other Notion features.
options.explanation: Comments complement, not replace, other features.

questions.question: If a user wants to explore pre‑made structures for handling comments and discussions, which resource does the article suggest?

options.text: The Notion API documentation.
options.explanation: API docs are for developers, not template browsing.

options.text: The template gallery with over 10,000 templates.
options.explanation: The article points readers to the template gallery for ready‑made solutions.

options.text: The YouTube channel’s comment tutorials.
options.explanation: While helpful, the article explicitly mentions the template gallery for this purpose.

options.text: The "Help Center" FAQ page.
options.explanation: The FAQ is for general help, not a collection of templates.
